metadata.title: Claude Writes Your Prompt For You

beats.narration_text: The idea that Claude can draft my prompt for me is fascinating, but I want to understand what the metaprompt is actually doing rather than just copy its output. Take a vague task of mine, say classifying support tickets by urgency, and: (1) walk me through the structure the metaprompt generates, naming why each section is there, (2) show me two spots where its generated prompt is generic and I'd hand-tune it for my data, (3) generate a second version at a different specificity level so I can compare them.

props.command: The idea that Claude can draft my prompt for me is fascinating, but I want to understand what the metaprompt is actually doing rather than just copy its output. Take a vague task of mine, say classifying support tickets by urgency, and: (1) walk me through the structure the metaprompt generates, naming why each section is there, (2) show me two spots where its generated prompt is generic and I'd hand-tune it for my data, (3) generate a second version at a different specificity level so I can compare them.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The Metaprompt is a long, multi-shot prompt filled with half a dozen examples of well-engineered prompts across different task types. You feed it two things: a plain-English task description, and optionally a list of variable names in caps. Claude reads those examples, infers the structure your task needs, and writes the template. You don't touch the Metaprompt itself — it's the engine. You're just setting the destination.

props.artifactHeading: You describe the task — Claude writes the prompt
artifactLines: Input: plain-English task + optional variable names in CAPS
Engine: a long multi-shot prompt with 6 high-quality examples
Claude infers the structure your task requires
Output: full template with <Inputs>, chain-of-thought, and examples
You iterate from a solid baseline — not from nothing

beats.narration_text: The output has three sections. First, the Inputs block — it lists every variable Claude decided your prompt needs, formatted as curly-dollar-sign names. Second, the Instructions Structure — Claude's reasoning about what the prompt needs to accomplish and in what order. Third, the template itself: complete with XML tags separating your variable slots from the static instructions, and a chain-of-thought scaffold already written. That's three artifacts for one task description.

props.artifactHeading: One task description produces three structured artifacts
artifactLines: <Inputs>: all variables Claude chose — {$CUSTOMER_EMAIL}, {$COMPANY_NAME}
<Instructions Structure>: Claude's reasoning about what the prompt needs
<Prompt>: the full template with XML delimiters and variable slots
Chain-of-thought scaffold already written into the instructions
Few-shot examples inserted where the task benefits from them

beats.narration_text: Once you have the template, the notebook lets you test it immediately. You plug in example values for each variable — a real customer email, your company name — and run the generated prompt against a faster model. You see the output, compare it to what you'd have written by hand, and start iterating. The Metaprompt doesn't write a perfect prompt. It writes a testable prompt, which is a different and more useful thing.

props.artifactHeading: Plug in real values — run it — compare
artifactLines: Set VARIABLES = ['CUSTOMER_EMAIL', 'COMPANY_NAME']
Fill example values: a real complaint email + your brand name
Run against claude-haiku — fast, cheap, immediate feedback
Compare output to your hand-written draft
Iterate from evidence, not intuition

beats.narration_text: Two hard limits. First: the Metaprompt is designed for single-turn prompts only — one question, one answer. It won't help you design a multi-turn conversation or an agentic loop. Second: the output is a starting point, not a final product. Anthropic says this explicitly. The generated prompt isn't guaranteed to be optimal. What it does is eliminate the blank page and give you something concrete to break.

props.artifactHeading: Two hard limits — know them before you ship
artifactLines: Single-turn only: one prompt, one response — no multi-turn design
Not for agentic loops, tool use, or conversation flows
Output is not guaranteed optimal — it's a baseline for iteration
Anthropic says: 'Don't be afraid to change it'
The value is eliminating blank-page paralysis, not replacing craft

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Ask Claude: 'Act as the Metaprompt. My task is: write a product description for an e-commerce listing. My variables are PRODUCT_NAME and KEY_FEATURES. Write me a complete prompt template with XML delimiters and chain-of-thought instructions.' That prompt forces Claude to replicate the Metaprompt pattern from memory — you'll see exactly what structure it thinks matters for a copywriting task, which tells you what it would have built for you automatically. Compare the two outputs. Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: Act as the Metaprompt. Task: write a product description for an e-commerce listing. Variables: PRODUCT_NAME, KEY_FEATURES. Write a complete prompt template with XML delimiters and chain-of-thought instructions.
props.segment: Claude Writes Your Prompt For You
output: Claude generates <Inputs> block with your variable names
Produces full template with XML tags and chain-of-thought
